proof_tex: Fix \(t\in\mathcal T\), and define the conditional discrepancy\[D_t(x)=q_{t0}(x)-e_t(x,0)\mu_{0t}(x)-e_{1-t}(x,0)c_t(x).\]All conditional expectations below may be represented by arbitrary measurable versions; multiplying by the observational-study mass makes the conclusion invariant to their values off the support of \(X\mid S=0\). By conditional iterated expectation under \(Q\), using that \(A\in\{t,1-t\}\),\[\begin{aligned}q_{t0}(X)&=E_Q[Y(t)\mid X,S=0]\\&=Q(A=t\mid X,S=0)E_Q[Y(t)\mid X,S=0,A=t]\\&\quad+Q(A=1-t\mid X,S=0)E_Q[Y(t)\mid X,S=0,A=1-t]\end{aligned}\]almost surely on \(\{S=0\}\). Assumption \(\textup{ass:pushforward}\) identifies the conditional assignment probabilities in this display with \(e_t(X,0)\) and \(e_{1-t}(X,0)\). On \(\{S=0,A=t\}\), consistency gives \(Y(t)=Y(A)\), and the same pushforward identifies the first conditional mean with \(\mu_{0t}(X)\). The second conditional mean is \(c_t(X)\) by its definition. Hence \(D_t(X)=0\) \(Q\)-almost surely on \(\{S=0\}\). The pushforward also gives \(Q(S=0\mid X)=P(S=0\mid X)=1-\pi(X)\). Therefore\[E_{P_X}[(1-\pi(X))|D_t(X)|]=E_Q[1\{S=0\}|D_t(X)|]=0.\]The integrand is nonnegative, so \((1-\pi(X))D_t(X)=0\) \(P_X\)-almost surely. On \(\{x:\pi(x)<1\}\), the factor \(1-\pi(x)\) is strictly positive; division there yields \(D_t(x)=0\) \(P_X\)-almost surely. Since \(t\) was arbitrary and \(\mathcal T\) is finite, the conclusion holds for every \(t\in\mathcal T\).

Suppose, toward a contradiction, that \(C_P(\theta)>0\) and that some \(Q\in\mathcal M(P,\rho,\gamma)\) exists. By \(\textup{def:latent-class}\), this law satisfies \(\textup{ass:pushforward}\), \(\textup{ass:relative-nuc}\), and \(\textup{ass:study-exchangeability}\). By \(\textup{lem:observational-mixture-identity}\), for each \(t\in\mathcal T\),\[(1-\pi(X))\{q_{t0}(X)-e_t(X,0)\mu_{0t}(X)-e_{1-t}(X,0)c_t(X)\}=0\]almost surely. Assumption \(\textup{ass:uniform-study-positivity}\) gives \(1-\pi(X)\ge\varepsilon>0\), so division yields\[q_{t0}(X)=e_t(X,0)\mu_{0t}(X)+e_{1-t}(X,0)c_t(X)\qquad P_X\text{-almost surely}.\]Together with \(\textup{ass:uniform-study-positivity}\), assumption \(\textup{ass:uniform-treatment-positivity}\) gives \(P(S=0,A=k\mid X)=(1-\pi(X))e_k(X,0)\ge\varepsilon^2\) for each \(k\in\mathcal T\), so all arm-specific conditional means in this identity are defined on the relevant \(P_X\)-support. The binary-treatment probabilities are nonnegative and sum to one. Assumption \(\textup{ass:relative-nuc}\) and \(\textup{def:exact-endpoints}\) therefore imply, endpoint by endpoint,\[W_t^-(X;\rho)\le q_{t0}(X)\le W_t^+(X;\rho)\qquad P_X\text{-almost surely}.\]Assumption \(\textup{ass:study-exchangeability}\) simultaneously gives\[V_t^-(X;\gamma)\le q_{t0}(X)\le V_t^+(X;\gamma)\qquad P_X\text{-almost surely}.\]Consequently, if\[L_t(X;\theta)=\max\{V_t^-(X;\gamma),W_t^-(X;\rho)\},\qquad U_t(X;\theta)=\min\{V_t^+(X;\gamma),W_t^+(X;\rho)\},\]then \(L_t(X;\theta)\le q_{t0}(X)\le U_t(X;\theta)\) almost surely. Thus \((L_t-U_t)_+=0\) almost surely and \(E_{P_X}[(L_t-U_t)_+]=0\) for both treatment arms. Definition \(\textup{def:compatibility-functional}\) now gives \(C_P(\theta)=0\), contradicting \(C_P(\theta)>0\). Hence \(\mathcal M(P,\rho,\gamma)=\varnothing\).

Under \(P^{\mathrm{can}}\), every listed mean belongs to \([1/4,8]\). For \(m\in[1/4,8]\), \(\nu_m\) has nonnegative weights summing to one, is supported on \(\{1/4,8\}\), and has mean \(m\); hence every displayed observed outcome kernel is valid. Moreover,\[\pi(x_j)=\sum_{t\in\mathcal T}P^{\mathrm{can}}(S=1,A=t\mid X=x_j)=\frac12,\]and, for every \(s,t,j\),\[e_t(x_j,s)=\frac{P^{\mathrm{can}}(S=s,A=t\mid X=x_j)}{P^{\mathrm{can}}(S=s\mid X=x_j)}=\frac{1/4}{1/2}=\frac12.\]At \(\rho=\gamma=1\), the published endpoints reduce to\[v_t^-=0,\qquad v_t^+=2\mu_{1t},\qquad w_t^-=\frac12\mu_{0t},\qquad w_t^+=\frac32\mu_{0t}.\]For arm \(0\), the trial and observational intervals at \(x_1\) are therefore \([0,1]\) and \([2,6]\), so\[H_0^{\mathrm{LRVP}}(x_1;(1,1))=\min\{1,6\}-\max\{0,2\}=-1.\]At \(x_2\) they are \([0,8]\) and \([2,6]\), so \(H_0^{\mathrm{LRVP}}(x_2;(1,1))=6-2=4\). It follows that\[G_0^{\mathrm{LRVP}}(1,1)=\frac45(-1)+\frac15(4)=0.\]For arm \(1\), both strata have published intervals \([0,2]\) and \([1/2,3/2]\), whence \(H_1^{\mathrm{LRVP}}=1\) pointwise and \(G_1^{\mathrm{LRVP}}(1,1)=1\). For the support-clipped endpoints, \(1-\rho=1-\gamma=0\) gives \(C_t^-=V_t^-=1/4\), whereas \(C_t^+=\min\{8,2\mu_{0t}\}\) and \(V_t^+=\min\{8,2\mu_{1t}\}\). Thus for arm \(0\),\[V_0(x_1)=[1/4,1],\qquad V_0(x_2)=[1/4,8],\qquad C_0(x_j)=[1/4,8].\]Since \(e_0=e_1=1/2\) and \(\mu_{00}=4\), both strata have\[W_0(x_j)=\left[\frac12(4)+\frac12\left(\frac14\right),\frac12(4)+\frac12(8)\right]=[17/8,6].\]For arm \(1\), both \(\mu_{01}\) and \(\mu_{11}\) equal one, and hence\[V_1(x_j)=C_1(x_j)=[1/4,2],\qquad W_1(x_j)=\left[\frac12(1)+\frac12\left(\frac14\right),\frac12(1)+\frac12(2)\right]=[5/8,3/2].\]The positive gap between the maximum lower endpoint and minimum upper endpoint is therefore \(17/8-1=9/8\) for arm \(0\) at \(x_1\), zero for arm \(0\) at \(x_2\), and zero for arm \(1\) at either stratum. Definition \(\textup{def:compatibility-functional}\) yields\[C_{P^{\mathrm{can}}}(1,1)=\max\left\{\frac45\frac98+\frac15(0),0\right\}=\frac9{10}.\]Finally, because \(\varepsilon<1/2\), the equalities \(\pi=e_t=1/2\) satisfy both uniform positivity assumptions. The already proved \(\textup{prop:positive-violation-infeasibility}\) applies and gives \(\mathcal M(P^{\mathrm{can}},1,1)=\varnothing\), even though the published arm-zero signed average equals zero.

Fix \(t\in\mathcal T\). By the declared range of the observed conditional-mean nuisances, \(\mu_{0t}(X),\mu_{1t}(X)\in[a,b]\) \(P_X\)-almost surely. Therefore, at \(\rho=0\), the clipped missing-arm endpoints in \(\textup{def:exact-endpoints}\) satisfy
\[
C_t^-(X;0)=\max\{a,\mu_{0t}(X)\}=\mu_{0t}(X),\qquad C_t^+(X;0)=\min\{b,\mu_{0t}(X)\}=\mu_{0t}(X)
\]
\(P_X\)-almost surely. Similarly, at \(\gamma=0\),
\[
V_t^-(X;0)=V_t^+(X;0)=\mu_{1t}(X).
\]
Because \(e_t(X,0)\) and \(e_{1-t}(X,0)\) are the two conditional treatment probabilities for the binary treatment, \(e_t(X,0)+e_{1-t}(X,0)=1\). Substituting the preceding equality for \(C_t^-\) and \(C_t^+\) into the definition of \(W_t^-\) and \(W_t^+\) gives
\[
W_t^-(X;0)=W_t^+(X;0)=\bigl(e_t(X,0)+e_{1-t}(X,0)\bigr)\mu_{0t}(X)=\mu_{0t}(X).
\]
Consequently, \(\textup{def:compatibility-functional}\) yields
\[
\begin{aligned}
C_P(0,0)
&=\max_{t\in\mathcal T}E_{P_X}\!\left[\left(\max\{\mu_{1t}(X),\mu_{0t}(X)\}-\min\{\mu_{1t}(X),\mu_{0t}(X)\}\right)_+\right]\\
&=\max_{t\in\mathcal T}E_{P_X}\!\left[\left|\mu_{1t}(X)-\mu_{0t}(X)\right|\right].
\end{aligned}
\]
Here the second equality uses \(\max\{u,v\}-\min\{u,v\}=|u-v|\ge0\). Since \(\mathcal T=\{0,1\}\), the maximum of the two nonnegative expectations is zero if and only if each expectation is zero. A nonnegative random variable has expectation zero if and only if it is zero almost surely; hence
\[
C_P(0,0)=0
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mu_{0t}(X)=\mu_{1t}(X)\quad P_X\text{-almost surely for every }t\in\mathcal T.
\]
Finally, the definition \(\mathcal K(P)=\{\theta\in\Theta:C_P(\theta)=0\}\) implies \((0,0)\in\mathcal K(P)\) if and only if \(C_P(0,0)=0\), which proves the claimed equivalence.

We first verify membership and compute the target. The chosen study and treatment probabilities equal \(1/2\), so both positivity assumptions hold for every \(\varepsilon<1/2\). The covariate density is one. The endpoint law of \(Y\) is \(
u_m\) with \(m=m_0+(b-a)u(x)\) in cell \(S=1,A=0\) and \(m=m_0\) elsewhere. Choosing the radius of the \(C^ eta\)-ball for \(u\) sufficiently small keeps every mean in \([a,b]\) and every nuisance in the prescribed Hölder ball. Thus these laws form an admissible specialization of \(\overline{\mathcal P}\).

At \(ho=\gamma=0\), support clipping is inactive because every conditional mean belongs to \([a,b]\), and the binary-treatment identity \(e_0(x,s)+e_1(x,s)=1\) gives\[C_t^-(x;0)=C_t^+(x;0)=\mu_{0t}(x),\quad V_t^-(x;0)=V_t^+(x;0)=\mu_{1t}(x),\quad W_t^-(x;0)=W_t^+(x;0)=\mu_{0t}(x).\]Consequently, the conditional violation for arm \(t\) is \(\lvert\mu_{1t}(x)-\mu_{0t}(x)vert\). Only arm zero differs in the submodel, whence\[C_{P_u}(0,0)=(b-a)\int_{[0,1]^d}|u(x)|\,dx.\]All four faces and the positive part tie on the whole covariate support at \(u=0\), so the tie has positive, indeed unit, \(P_X\)-mass.

We next prove the lower bound by an explicit mixture. Choose a nonzero nonnegative \(C^\infty\) function \(\psi\), supported in the interior of \([0,1]^d\), with \(\lVert\psiVert_\infty\le1\). For a small bandwidth \(h\), pack \(M\asymp h^{-d}\) disjoint cubes of side \(h\) into \([0,1]^d\), with lower corners \(x_1,\ldots,x_M\). For \(\omega=(\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_M)\in\{-1,1\}^M\), set\[u_\omega(x)=c h^ eta\sum_{j=1}^M\omega_j\psi\!\left( rac{x-x_j}{h}ight).\]The supports are disjoint. The scaling identity for derivatives and the Hölder remainder show directly that \(\lVert u_\omegaVert_{C^ eta}\le C_\psi c\), uniformly in \(h\) and \(\omega\). Taking \(c\) small therefore places every alternative in the submodel and in \([-1/4,1/4]\). Moreover,\[\lVert u_\omegaVert_1=c h^ eta M h^d\int|\psi(z)|\,dz\asymp c h^ eta.\]

Let \(L_\omega=dP_{u_\omega}/dP_0\) for one observation. Conditional on \(G=1\{S=1,A=0\}\), the Bernoulli likelihood ratio is \(1+2(2Z-1)u_\omega(X)\), and it is one when \(G=0\). Since \(P_0(G=1)=1/4\), \(E_{P_0}(2Z-1)=0\), and \((2Z-1)^2=1\), direct multiplication gives\[E_{P_0}[L_\omega L_{\omega'}]=1+\langle u_\omega,u_{\omega'}angle_2.\]Let \(\overline P_n=2^{-M}\sum_\omega P_{u_\omega}^{\otimes n}\). Orthogonality of the disjoint bumps gives, for a constant \(\lambda=c^2\int\psi^2\),\[\langle u_\omega,u_{\omega'}angle_2=\lambda h^{2 eta+d}\sum_{j=1}^M\omega_j\omega_j'.\]Using \((1+x)^n\le e^{nx}\), averaging independently over \(\omega\) and \(\omega'\), and using \(\log\cosh z\le z^2/2\), we obtain\[1+\chi^2(\overline P_n,P_0^{\otimes n})\le\left\{\cosh igl(n\lambda h^{2 eta+d} igr)ight\}^M\le\exp\left( rac{M n^2\lambda^2h^{4 eta+2d}}2ight)\le\exp(Cc^4n^2h^{4 eta+d}).\]Take \(h\asymp n^{-2/(4 eta+d)}\). Then the last exponent is bounded by a constant multiple of \(c^4\), while every alternative has \(C_{P_{u_\omega}}(0,0)\asymp c(b-a)r_n\). By choosing \(c=c_\eta\) sufficiently small, the total-variation inequality \(\lVert\overline P_n-P_0^{\otimes n}Vert_{\mathrm{TV}}\le\sqrt{\chi^2(\overline P_n,P_0^{\otimes n})}/2\) makes this distance at most \(\eta+o(1)\). Therefore\[2^{-M}\sum_\omega E_{P_{u_\omega}}\varphi_n\le E_{P_0}\varphi_n+\eta+o(1)\le\alpha+\eta+o(1),\]so at least one mixture component has the asserted low power. If instead \(h\asymp n^{-1/(2 eta)}\), then the signal is of order \(n^{-1/2}\) and \(n^2h^{4 eta+d}=n^{-d/(2 eta)}	o0\). The same calculation gives total variation tending to zero and hence power at most \(\alpha+o(1)\) for at least one root-\(n\) alternative.

It remains to prove attainability of the rate. Define the projection kernel \(K_K(x,x')=\sum_{k=1}^Kp_k(x)p_k(x')\). The inverse-cell weight in \(R_i\) gives\[E_{P_u}[R_i\mid X_i=x]=4\left\{ rac14\left( rac12+u(x)ight)- rac14\left( rac12ight)ight\}=u(x).\]Independence of two distinct observations therefore yields\[E_{P_u}[\widehat Q_K]=\sum_{k=1}^K\langle u,p_kangle_2^2=\lVert\Pi_KuVert_2^2.\]A piecewise Taylor polynomial of degree \(\lfloor etafloor\) on cubes of side proportional to \(K^{-1/d}\) has pointwise remainder at most \(C K^{- eta/d}\) by the defining Hölder inequality. Since the orthogonal projection is the best \(L_2\) approximation,\[\lVert u-\Pi_KuVert_2\le C K^{- eta/d}.\]Also \(|R_i|\le4\). Writing the order-two statistic as its first projection plus a degenerate remainder and expanding the variance gives\[\operatorname{Var}_{P_u}(\widehat Q_K)\le C\left\{ rac{\lVert\Pi_KuVert_2^2}{n}+ rac{E[K_K(X_1,X_2)^2]}{n^2}ight\}=C\left\{ rac{\lVert\Pi_KuVert_2^2}{n}+ rac K{n^2}ight\}.\]The equality uses orthonormality twice: \(E[K_K(X_1,X_2)^2]=K\). Under \(u=0\), Chebyshev's inequality thus makes the rejection probability at most \(C/L_\alpha^2\), so a fixed sufficiently large \(L_\alpha\) gives level at most \(\alpha\). On a unit-volume cube, \(\lVert uVert_2\ge\lVert uVert_1\). With \(K\asymp n^{2d/(4 eta+d)}\),\[K^{- eta/d}\asymp r_n,\qquad  rac{\sqrt K}{n}\asymp r_n^2.\]Hence \(\lVert uVert_1/r_n	o\infty\) implies \(E\widehat Q_K/(\sqrt K/n)	o\infty\), and the displayed variance bound plus Chebyshev's inequality gives rejection probability tending to one uniformly. This proves the matching upper bound and the lemma.

The target calculation in \(\textup{lem:tie-subexperiment-minimax-boundary}\) gives\[C_{P_{u_n}}(0,0)=(b-a)|u_n|=\frac{|\kappa|}{\sqrt n}.\]Let \(G_1=1\{S=1,A=0\}\) and \(G_0=1\{S=0,A=0\}\). Since both cells have probability \(1/4\),\[E_{P_{u_n}}D_i=4(b-a)\left\{\frac14u_n-\frac14\cdot0\right\}=\frac{\kappa}{\sqrt n}.\]Because \(Z_i\in\{0,1\}\), \((Z_i-1/2)^2=1/4\) identically. The cells are disjoint and have total probability \(1/2\), so\[E_{P_{u_n}}D_i^2=16(b-a)^2\cdot\frac12\cdot\frac14=2(b-a)^2=\sigma^2.\]Thus \(\operatorname{Var}_{P_{u_n}}(D_i)\to\sigma^2\), and \(|D_i|\le2(b-a)\). For completeness, write \(X_{ni}=(D_i-E D_i)/(\sigma\sqrt n)\). Uniform boundedness gives \(\max_i|X_{ni}|\to0\), while \(\sum_iE X_{ni}^2\to1\). Taylor expansion \(E e^{itX_{ni}}=1-t^2E[X_{ni}^2]/2+o(E[X_{ni}^2])\), uniformly in \(i\), and multiplication over the independent observations show that the characteristic function of \(\sum_iX_{ni}\) converges to \(e^{-t^2/2}\). Therefore\[\frac{\sqrt n}{\sigma}\left(\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^nD_i-\frac{\kappa}{\sqrt n}\right)\rightsquigarrow N(0,1),\]which is the asserted shifted-normal limit. Taking the probability of the event \(|Z+\kappa/\sigma|>z_{1-\alpha/2}\) for \(Z\sim N(0,1)\) yields the displayed power formula.
